session_notes.note: I smoke bidis. About 25 a day for the last 45 years. I've reduced a little now because the doctor here keeps telling me to.
I wake up early, go out to the fields, work all morning. Sometimes if I'm feeling stressed about the crops or if it's too quiet, I... I might have one or two before I start work.
It's both, I think. Being alone in the fields, it's quiet, and if the crops are not doing well... it makes me feel anxious.
That might make it easier. I like working in the fields, doing something productive instead of smoking.
Yes, I'll feel proud of myself, I guess. I'll think "I did it, I worked without smoking."
Morning's the hardest. Right after waking up, I feel like I need one.
I smoke bidis. One packet a day, about 15 to 16. I've been doing this since I was around 18 or 20.
When I start my shift, my coworkers usually have bidis to share, so I smoke one then.
My wife, she's been telling me it's bad for our health, and our finances aren't doing great, so maybe I can save some money if I stop.
They'll probably laugh and say it won't last. They've tried quitting before too, but they never follow through.
Not much, I guess I just couldn't resist when everyone was smoking around me. Maybe I need to figure out a way to avoid those situations.
When we go for a break, they usually offer me one and I sit with them to smoke. I'd say...I'd say something like "not today, got to get back to work" or something like that.
I smoke bidis and eat paan. Started when I was around 25. Now it's maybe 7 or 8 bidis a day, down from more before.
I feel... irritable. Like I'm waiting for something. Can't concentrate. It's hard to explain. It's like my body's telling me to have paan or a bidi.

My daughter... she said it's bad for my health. I'm having some trouble breathing in the cold. And I'm 62, not as fit as I used to be. Maybe quitting will help me breathe better. But it's hard. It's a habit, you know? Been doing it for so long.
It sounds... simple. I'll try it. But what if it doesn't work? What if I still feel like having a bidi or paan?
I don't know... it's just that I've been doing this for so long. I'm not sure I can stop. It's like... what will I do without it? I feel lonely enough already.
I don't know... it's hard to let go of that. My wife was very encouraging, always telling me how good I was. It's been a long time since I've had anyone to share it with.
I'll try it. But what if I'm not strong enough? What if I still go for a bidi after 10 minutes?

past_strategies.strategy: went a week or two without smoking

session_notes.note: My son is being treated here, so I thought I should see someone too. I smoke when I get angry or stressed. It's hard to explain.
About 12 years now, yeah. It's just become a habit for me, I guess.
I don't know, I guess I just feel like I'm not setting a good example for him. That's all.
Well, I guess I would smoke less during the summer breaks when I was planning something outdoors with the family. It wasn't like I stopped completely, but maybe I'd go a week or two without smoking.
But how am I supposed to plan a fun activity when I'm sitting here talking about quitting? It's hard to get excited about anything when I'm feeling anxious and stressed about my son's treatment.
.. I guess just taking a few deep breaths might help. But it's not the same as actually quitting, is it?

The summer breaks... I think what was different back then was that I wasn't as anxious or stressed about things. I could just focus on the kids and having fun. Maybe if I could find a way to manage my anxiety better, I could quit for good.
I guess it might feel kind of... calm, you know? Like I'd be taking care of myself, even if it's just for a few minutes each day. Okay, I'll try it.
Honestly, I don't know why I'm here. I don't think smoking is a big deal. I've been doing it for 25 years and I'm fine.
Boredom in the shop when I'm waiting for customers. That's usually when I get a cigarette. And sometimes after chai in the morning, I light one up. It's just something I do, you know?

To be honest, I think I'd just be missing my routine. It's like a little break for me, you know? I'd have to find something else to do in the shop when I'm bored. Maybe I could read a book or something. But I don't know, it's not like I'm really attached to the cigarettes themselves, it's just what I do.
That sounds okay, I guess. I mean, I like pastries, so that's not a bad idea. But what if I have a bad day and want to smoke anyway? Will that be a problem?

Reminding myself why I'm quitting? That's my wife's idea, right? She wants me to quit so I'll live longer and not die on her. I'm not sure that's going to be enough to keep me from smoking when I'm feeling stressed.

Honestly? I don't know. I've never really thought about it. Maybe I'd be more patient with customers, or something. I don't know, I've never really tried to handle stress without smoking. My wife says I get more angry and short-tempered when I'm stressed and smoking, but I don't know if I feel that way inside.

That sounds like a good idea. A week without smoking, plus a week of taking breaks outside, and then I can treat myself to a nice cup of coffee from that new cafe down the street. I like their coffee.
I've been smoking for 20 years and I've tried to quit many times. I don't know why I'm even here. It never works out.

It's just that life is busier now, more stressful, and I don't have as much time for myself. I feel like I'm already overwhelmed, and quitting smoking just feels like one more thing I have to deal with. It's nice to just have a cigarette after a long day to unwind.
I'll try it, but can it be a private reminder, just for me? I don't want my coworkers to know I'm trying to quit.
It's weird, just talking about this makes me feel a little better. I guess I thought I'd always be stuck this way, but maybe I don't have to be.

That sounds... okay, I guess. It's just that it's hard to see how making a schedule for reports is going to help me not smoke, you know? But I'll try it. What's the point of doing anything if I'm just going to go back to smoking afterwards?
A walk sounds nice, I guess. But what if I'm in a meeting or something and I need to go outside? Do I just leave the meeting?
Honestly, I've always turned to cigarettes when I'm bored or need a break, and I'm worried that eating more snacks will make me gain weight. I'm really scared of that.

I don't know... I just see myself getting bigger, feeling self-conscious all the time, and not being able to fit into my clothes anymore. It's like, what's the point of quitting smoking if I'm just going to end up miserable in a different way?
Yeah, okay. I'll try it. It can't hurt, right? But if I start gaining weight...

motivation_reason: hard work doesn't feel so bad

session_notes.note: Twelve, maybe a bit more. Sometimes I have a few bidis too, if we're really busy.

It's just busy, you know? We're slammed with orders, the kitchen's a mess... it's chaos. But it's a good kind of chaos. All my mates are smoking, laughing, making jokes... it's like our own little bubble.
Yeah... I guess it's just the camaraderie. We all come together, share a laugh, and for a second, all the hard work doesn't feel so bad.

Yeah, that sounds doable. Maybe I can ask him to call me every day after lunch, when things get really busy. That way, he can remind me that I'm quitting and... it might just keep me from reaching for a cigarette.
Yeah, I'll do it. I'll ask him as soon as I leave here. Thanks... I guess I was more ready to quit than I thought.
I quit for about 3 months last year. Then a really bad week at work happened and I started again. Now I'm back to 15 a day.

Honestly, it's just hard to believe in myself after relapsing. I thought I was over it, but I guess I was just putting it off, waiting for the "right" moment. And like you said, I know my triggers, but it's hard to think I can handle a crisis situation without smoking.

That makes sense, I guess. I mean, I've tried to just "not think about it" before, and that never works. Having a plan, yeah, that sounds like a good idea. But what if the situation is just too bad? Like, I'm in a meeting and my boss is yelling at me? What's my plan then?

I usually just try to tune out, zone out, and get through the meeting as quickly as possible. Sometimes I'll even grab a cigarette as soon as it's over, just to calm down, I guess. But that doesn't really help in the long run, does it?

I don't know...it sounds kind of silly, setting a timer and all. But at the same time, it's not like I have anything to lose, right? And maybe it would actually help. Okay, fine. I'll try it. But if I'm still stuck in that meeting after 10 minutes, and my boss is still yelling at me, I'm not promising anything.

It's just...I don't want to be a "quitter" again. I want to prove to myself that I can do this for real this time. And I guess talking about it with you makes me feel like I'm actually taking a step towards being able to quit.

I guess I'm worried about relapsing again because the last time, I only made it 3 months, and then something big came up and I just caved. This time, I feel like I'm older, wiser, and more prepared. Maybe we can use that as a strength, instead of just focusing on what went wrong. I mean, I've been to this point before, I know what works for me, and I can build on that, right?

I think I can use my experience to anticipate what might trigger me to smoke, and have a plan in place to deal with it before it becomes a problem. Like, if I know I'm going to have a stressful meeting, I can prepare myself beforehand, take some deep breaths, and remind myself that I can get through it. And if I do start to feel overwhelmed, I can use the 10-minute timer technique we talked about. It's not just about quitting smoking, it's about learning to handle stress in a healthier way. And I think I'm ready for that.
I smoke because it calms me down when I feel anxious. I know it makes things worse but I can't stop.
About five years, I guess. It just started as a habit with friends at first, and then I kept doing it even when they stopped.
Delaying my response to a craving doesn't really help me when it's about my anxiety, you know? It feels like I'm just putting off dealing with it, not really fixing it.
My friends just tell me to quit already. They don't really listen or try to understand. It's always like that.
I don't know... my partner smokes too. It's hard to imagine quitting without them.
I guess I could try talking to them about it. But if they're not willing to quit themselves, it doesn't really feel like they'll be very supportive of me quitting.
I don't know, it feels kinda weird to say no to them. We always do it together when we hang out.
I don't know... that sounds kinda silly. It's not the same as saying no when we're out drinking or something.

I guess... like if we were at a bar and they were offering me a cigarette, I would try to say something like, "No thanks, I'm trying to quit" or something, but it feels fake if I've only practiced it in a normal conversation about food or something.
That might actually be kind of helpful. I never thought about it, but I do always light a cigarette right after we're done eating. It's just something I've always done.

motivation_reason: see things a little more clearly

session_notes.note: I had quit for 2 full years. Then I went to a party, had a few drinks, and one of my friends offered a cigarette. That was 6 months ago. Now I'm back to 10-15 a day.

I just thought it was no big deal, you know? A party, a few drinks, the whole thing. It was like old times. But since then, I've been smoking more than ever. It's like I got stuck in a rut and can't find a way out.

It's the feeling of disappointment in myself, I guess. I know I can quit, I've done it before. But now it feels like I'm just going through the motions, you know? It's like I'm not even trying to change. The one thing... I don't know if I can even say it out loud... but I guess it's the drinking and smoking together. I love going out with my friends, having a beer, but if I have to quit smoking, is that going to ruin that for me?

Yeah, I guess I'm worried about that. If I quit smoking, will my friends still want to hang out with me? But at the same time, I really don't want to be that guy who's always coughing and smelling like smoke. As for enjoying time with friends without smoking... I don't know, maybe just finding other ways to enjoy myself? Like, playing video games with them instead of standing outside smoking? Or finding other hobbies to do together? I don't know, it's all just a bit daunting.
A short walk outside might be okay, I guess. But what if my friends think it's weird? What if they're all standing there smoking and I'm just walking back and forth by myself?

I don't know... I'd probably just say something like "no thanks, I'm trying to quit" or something lame like that. But I hate saying no to my friends, especially when they're offering me a cigarette. It feels like I'm letting them down or something. 

And then they'd be like "oh, come on, just one won't hurt" and I'd be like "fine" and then I'd be right back to where I started. It's a vicious cycle.

Yeah, that sounds like a decent way to say no. I mean, it shows them that I'm not just quitting because of some vague health reasons, but because I'm actually serious about it. And it gives them an out, you know? Like, they can't say "oh, come on, just one won't hurt" if I'm being firm but polite. I think I could try that.

The morning cravings are the worst. I think I'll need to stay really busy in the mornings, whether it's going for a run or doing some work. And maybe having a cup of coffee or something right when I wake up could help take the edge off. I also think I might need to find some new morning habits, like meditating or something, to replace the old habit of smoking.

Yeah, that sounds like a good idea. Having a daily routine would give me some structure and make it easier to stick to my quit plan. I like the idea of scheduling specific activities for the morning, like taking a walk or doing some stretching. That way, I'll have something to look forward to and it'll help me stay on track.

Yeah, I feel like I'm finally getting some momentum going. I'm really glad we've had this conversation, it's helped me see things a little more clearly. I'm ready to move forward with this quit plan and make some real changes.
I've tried to quit 3 times. The longest I went was 3 months. But I just can't seem to stay quit. My daughter is growing up and I don't want her to see me like this.
It's the stress at work, that's my main problem. Meetings and all that. When I'm stuck in traffic driving to or from work, that's when I crave a cigarette the most.
That sounds like a good idea, but how am I supposed to take deep breaths when I'm in the middle of rush hour traffic? It's not exactly the most peaceful place.
I guess I could listen to a podcast or a guided meditation on my phone. I've done that before, but it's hard to focus when you're stuck in traffic.
I just don't know if it's going to be enough to actually stop me from smoking when I finally can get out of the car. What if it doesn't work?

Okay... I guess I was thinking about those long meetings on Fridays. My boss likes to go on and on about sales numbers and it's always right before lunch when I feel like I need a cigarette the most. If I can tap my foot in time with my pen, maybe I can get through the meeting without lighting up.

He's a social smoker, always lighting up at family gatherings or when we're out with friends. And sometimes he'll even smoke in the house. I've been trying to get him to quit for years, but he just says it's no big deal and that he can quit anytime he wants to. But I know he's been smoking for a long time and it's hard for him to stop.
I guess I could try explaining to him how hard it is for me to quit and ask him to please consider quitting too, for my sake. Maybe I could even offer to help him quit if he wants to.
I chew gutkha, not cigarettes. About 8 to 10 pouches a day for the last 7 years. My mouth has developed some kind of white patch and the doctor is worried.
Actually, I mostly chew it while driving. During long trips, at red lights, and even sometimes when I'm waiting for passengers. It's just a habit by now, I guess. Help me stay awake and focused.

That sounds a bit difficult. I mean, I'd have to stop and get out of my rickshaw at a red light, right? Wouldn't that be a hassle? And when I'm waiting for passengers, I'm usually parked in a crowded area. Can't just get out and stretch there.

It's just... I don't know, I guess it's the waiting and the boredom. When I'm driving, I'm always on the go, but when I'm stopped, there's just this emptiness. And my hands are just sitting there, waiting for me to do something with them. I think that's part of it.

I was thinking maybe I could keep a small notebook in my rickshaw, and just draw something whenever I feel like it. Doesn't have to be at the same time every day, just whenever I'm feeling bored or restless. And maybe I can even try to draw something specific, like the people around me or the rickshaw itself. Something to distract me, you know?

motivation_reason: showing them I care about my health

session_notes.note: I started smoking 2 years ago in college. My friends all do it. I'm on the cricket team and my stamina is getting really bad now.
I guess it means I'm accepted and can hang out with them without being judged... or so I think. Quitting would be like, I don't know, showing them that I care about my health over our hangouts.

Honestly, it's kinda made me feel like I'm not cool. Like, Rohan's always there, preaching about how bad smoking is, and I'm the one who's actually doing it, but then I'm the one who's not cool because I don't smoke.

That sounds kinda hard, to be honest. I mean, how am I supposed to avoid the guys just because they're smoking? That's like, a huge part of our hangouts. And what about when we have competitions, like matches? They're always smoking and stuff before and after.
I guess that's not a bad idea. But what if they don't want to do that? Or what if I'm the only one who wants to stop smoking and it's just awkward for everyone else?

That actually sounds kinda cool. I used to draw a lot when I was younger, maybe that's something I can get back into. But what about when I'm hanging out with the guys and they're all smoking? I don't want to stand out even more by drawing or using a stress ball.
My son brought me here. I smoke 4-5 bidis a day and eat paan. I've been doing this for 30 years. I don't think I can stop at this age.
After cooking, I often take a break and light a bidi. It's a habit. During rest time in the afternoons, I also sometimes smoke. It's just something I do.
He's worried about me having trouble breathing when I walk. He says I should stop smoking. I know he's right, but what's the point at my age?
I like taking a short walk, but will I be able to stop after cooking? It's hard to stop myself from reaching for a bidi. Maybe I can try the walk, but what if I just feel like having a bidi instead?
Paan is a part of our custom. My mother-in-law used to give it to my husband, and I started eating it with him. It's hard to stop because it feels like part of our tradition.
I never thought of that. We could take a walk together after lunch instead of eating paan. It's something we can do together, like before.

I think it will help me remember that there's something else I can do instead of paan. And when I'm stressed, walking with my husband will make me feel better. It's not just about quitting paan, it's about spending time with my husband and taking care of myself.
I think I'll need some reminders. Maybe I can put a note on the kitchen counter or a picture of my grandkids near the front door to remind me to go for a walk instead of paan.
I smoke about 15 cigarettes a day. And I drink on weekends. When I drink, the smoking goes up a lot. My wife has been asking me to stop both.

Well, it's like... after a few drinks, I feel more stressed, you know? And then I start thinking about work and all the stuff I've been putting off, and that's when I usually have a cigarette to calm down.

I don't know... I've tried to stop smoking before, and it's not like this is a new idea. But I do get anxious, especially when I'm drinking, and just standing outside won't make the stress go away, will it?
I suppose that could work, maybe. But what if I'm with friends and they're all smoking? I don't want to be the loser who doesn't join in, you know?

My wife would definitely support me, but it's not like she can be with me all the time. And my friends... I don't know, I feel like they'd just tease me if I didn't have a cigarette when they're smoking.
That's not a bad idea. I can ask him to be my quit buddy. But what about when I'm stressed at work? How can I deal with that?

I can try that. It's not like I've never done it before, but maybe this time it'll stick. And if I'm really stressed, maybe I can take a short break and call my brother instead of grabbing a cigarette.
That might work. I mean, it's silly, but it's something to do with my hands, right? And if I'm talking to my brother, maybe I won't even think about smoking.

motivation_reason: being a good role model for them
fun night in with a movie

session_notes.note: I've been smoking for about 2 years. My husband and I are planning to have a baby soon. I know I need to stop.
Work breaks. Usually when I'm stressed about lesson plans or grading. And after dinner, I've been having just one cigarette.
That sounds like a good idea. I can try to distract myself. But what about when I'm stressed at work? I won't be able to take a walk or brush my teeth in the middle of a meeting.
Deep breaths, yeah... I can do that. But what about if I'm having a really tough day and I just feel like I need a cigarette?
I guess I would say something like, "No thanks, I'm trying to quit." But it's hard when it feels like everyone else is smoking and I'll be the only one who doesn't.

I guess I could say like, "Thanks for offering, but I'm trying to cut back on smoking" or "I'm working on quitting, so I'll pass on the cigarette." That way, I'm being honest but not really making a big deal out of it.

I think it'll be a good reminder of why I'm doing this. Like, if I'm feeling stressed or anxious and wanting to smoke, thinking about the baby and being a good role model for them will help me stay on track.
I like that idea. I could reward myself with a nice dinner or a massage, or even just a fun night in with a movie. That would be a good motivator.

motivation_reason: finding ways to manage my distractions

session_notes.note: I work from home and since 2020 my smoking has more than doubled. I used to smoke 5 a day and now it's 18-20.

It's just so easy to get distracted, you know? I'll be working on a task and then my wife will call or I'll get a notification on my phone and I'll take a break to smoke. And then I'll think, "Oh, I'll just quickly check something online" and before I know it, 20 minutes have passed and I've smoked another cigarette.

I've heard of that technique before, and I think it's interesting. But don't you think it's just delaying the inevitable? I mean, I'll still end up smoking, it's just pushing the problem to a later time. Can't we work on a more long-term solution, like finding ways to manage my distractions or changing my habits entirely?

Chewing gum might be alright, I suppose. But deep breathing? I'm not so sure. I mean, I've tried meditation and stuff like that before, but it's hard for me to focus on my breathing when I'm feeling stressed or bored. Unless... unless you have some specific technique or trick to help me do it?

That sounds like a more concrete plan, I guess. But what about when I'm stuck on a problem and I really need a break? Won't the breathing thing just make me more anxious? Or, like, more aware of how anxious I am?

Wait, so you're saying I should do something like take a short walk or brush my teeth within 10 minutes of finishing a meal, like a new habit to replace smoking. That's actually kind of a good idea... I mean, I've always brushed my teeth after meals, but I never thought to link it to quitting smoking.

Yeah, I think I can try that. But can I ask, what about my wife's pregnancy? Shouldn't I be doing more for her sake, too? I mean, I know I want to quit smoking for my own health, but isn't this the right time to do it for her, too?

I think that's a good compromise. Yeah, I can do that. It's not like I have to show it to anyone, right? But, um, what about my cravings when I'm not walking or after dinner? Do I just... deal with those on my own?

Talking to someone about my cravings, huh? That's a little weird, but I guess it could help. I mean, I could text my wife or a friend and just say, "Hey, I'm having a craving, can you talk to me about something?" or something. Would that even work?
I used to smoke one and a half bundles of bidis a day. That's about 25. Now I'm down to 2 or 3. My doctor says I should stop completely.

You know, it was usually when I was working, especially when I was dealing with a lot of paperwork and had to meet deadlines. I'd get frustrated or stressed, and that's when I'd feel like having a bidi. Also, sometimes with friends or family, like when we're eating dinner or watching a movie, they'd offer me one, and I'd give in. Not anymore, though – it's just become a habit, I guess, this last one in the morning after waking up.

A short walk sounds good, but I'm not sure about the stretching exercises – I'm not very flexible. But yeah, a walk might help. It's just that this last bidi is the hardest one to give up, you know? It's like my brain is telling me, "You've made it this far, you can take one more."

I'm willing to try it, but what if it rains? I don't like walking in the rain. And can't I just start with a shorter walk, like to the kitchen, and see how it goes? I don't want to commit to a whole 5-10 minutes if it feels pointless.

Yeah, I guess it would be better than nothing. And if it feels too easy, I can always try a longer walk later. But what if I have to make a cup of tea or something before I go? That would be a bit of a barrier.
Yeah, I can try. It's not like I have to finish the job, just do a little bit for a few minutes. But what if I get caught up in it and forget about quitting?
I'll try it. I'll set the timer for 10 minutes and see how it goes. If I can get through the paperwork without smoking, that's a start, right?
I smoke about 20 cigarettes a day. I calculate I spend about 4000 rupees a month on cigarettes. My family is struggling and I feel guilty about it.
It's always there, in the back of my mind. I don't like feeling guilty all the time, it's hard to enjoy my time with the kids when I'm thinking about how much I'm spending on cigarettes.
I usually have a cup of chai before I head out for delivery. Sometimes I'll smoke before that, just to calm my nerves a bit. It's become automatic, I don't even think about it most days.

I guess it's not like I'll be running late by just 10 minutes. But still, every minute counts when I'm driving around the city. Okay, fine, I'll try it. But if I'm really going to be late, I'm going back to smoking.

I suppose it's worth a shot. I've tried quitting before and ended up going back to 20 a day. This time, I'm trying to look at it like a business expense reduction. Can you help me calculate how much I'll save if I reduce to 10 a day?

If I save 300 rupees a day by quitting 10 cigarettes, that's 2100 rupees a week. I could put that money towards my kids' school fees. I was thinking, maybe I could start by reducing my morning cigarettes by 1 or 2. Then see how it goes from there.

motivation_reason: missing out on the whole experience
being a better role model for my friends

session_notes.note: I only smoke at parties or when I'm drinking with friends, maybe 3-4 times a week. It's not like I'm a real smoker. But I've been having asthma attacks more often.
Honestly, it was just feeling like everyone around me was smoking and it made social gatherings feel weird if I didn't have a cigarette too.

I guess that could work, but it's not like I have a whole social calendar of non-smoking events or friends already. And what if I go to a party and all my friends are smoking? It feels like that's just impossible to avoid.

Polite excusing myself could work, I guess. But what if they all come outside to join me? And distracting myself with a friend... if they're all smoking, I'll just feel left out. It's hard to think about quitting when it feels like I'll be missing out on the whole experience.

My mom's been nagging me to quit for years, but she's really supportive. She wouldn't offer me a cigarette even if she was smoking. I could ask her to come with me to parties, at least, so she could keep me company and remind me why I'm quitting.

It feels a little weird to be so serious about quitting, but talking to you has made it feel more doable, I guess. I'm starting to see it as a way to take care of my health, which is pretty important to me.

That's not a bad idea. If I write down my reasons, like taking care of my asthma and being a better role model for my friends, maybe it'll help me remember why I'm quitting when I feel like having a cigarette.

It's just having a little reminder of why I'm going through all this. If I'm feeling anxious or tempted, I can just pull out my phone and read my reasons. It might help me feel more grounded and less likely to give in to my cravings.
Yeah, that sounds like it could work. Sometimes just getting up and moving around helps me feel better when I'm stressed out. And if it takes my mind off smoking, that's a bonus.

I do deep breathing exercises when I'm anxious or stressed at work, so it's definitely something that works for me. I can try doing that when I'm at a party or social situation and feeling the urge to smoke.
